macro_architecture.reasoning: 2 active containers (frontend, backend) with 951 total components. Patterns: e2e-xnp: Unknown, jsApi: Unknown, import-schema: Unknown, frontend: Component-Based (Angular), backend: Layered.

layer_structure: Adapter/Infrastructure Layer (Adapter services, API modules, HTTP interfaces)

container_analyses.summary: The frontend is a component‑based Angular application that implements the legal deed and document management domain through a layered architecture (presentation, service, and adapter/infrastructure). It leverages Angular, TypeScript, RxJS, Angular Material and a suite of RESTful APIs to deliver capabilities such as deed CRUD, document import/export, workflow execution, authentication, key management and reporting. While the codebase follows standard Angular patterns, the current structure shows moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion, with a high count of services, adapters, pipes, and components that introduce technical debt and maintenance overhead.

layers: Adapter/Infrastructure Layer (Adapter services, API modules, HTTP interfaces)

capabilities: Create, edit, view and delete deeds
Import and export documents (JSON, PDF, HTML)
Manage deed metadata and annotations
Execute workflow actions (archive, change, delete, re‑encryption)
Handle document archiving and custody operations
Perform user authentication and session management
Manage encryption keys and re‑encryption processes
Send and receive notifications
Generate and retrieve reports and analytics
Schedule and monitor background jobs and tasks
Search and filter deed / document data
Provide role‑based access control via guards
functional.bounded_context: Legal Deed & Document Management (Deed Registry) with integrated workflow and archiving

debt_indicators: Very large number of services (131) – risk of God‑service / mixed responsibilities
Big set of adapters (50) – may hide duplicated integration logic
67 pipes – possible over‑fragmentation and low reusability
Components count (128) suggests UI may be overly fine‑grained, increasing maintenance overhead
Potential missing domain‑level abstractions between adapters and services
recommendations: Refactor overly large services into smaller, focused services adhering to SRP
Introduce façade or domain‑specific service layers to decouple UI components from low‑level adapters
Consolidate similar pipes into shared utility pipes or move complex logic to services
Group related components, services, and adapters into feature modules to improve cohesion
Add explicit TypeScript interfaces for service contracts to reduce tight coupling
Review injection points to identify and eliminate unnecessary cross‑module dependencies

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The frontend meets functional requirements and follows a solid Angular foundation, but the high artifact count and moderate structural qualities indicate growing technical debt. Refactoring and better modularization are needed to sustain scalability and maintainability.
top_recommendations: Refactor overly large services into smaller, SRP‑compliant services.
Introduce façade/domain‑specific service layers to isolate UI components from low‑level adapters.
Consolidate and rationalize pipes and move complex transformations to services.

layers: Adapter/Infrastructure Layer (Adapter services, API modules, HTTP interfaces)

container_analyses.summary: The backend container follows a classic layered architecture using Spring Boot, exposing REST controllers that directly call service implementations which in turn access JPA repositories. It models a rich legal‑document lifecycle domain with over 300 entity classes and dozens of services, but suffers from moderate separation of concerns, coupling, and cohesion, leading to a grade of C. Key pain points include oversized services, leakage of DAO implementations into the service layer, lack of explicit interfaces, and missing DTO boundaries. Refactoring toward clearer aggregate roots, a dedicated use‑case layer, and stricter interface‑driven design will markedly improve maintainability and scalability.

capabilities: Create, read, update, delete (CRUD) deed entries and related registry data
Manage document metadata and versioning
Handle handover data sets and successor batches
Perform cryptographic key management and re‑encryption
Archive and re‑archive documents with signed tokens
Process actions and background jobs (scheduling, workers)
Log deed entry activity and generate reports
Apply correction notes to deeds
Validate and enforce user authorizations
Expose comprehensive REST APIs for external consumption
Health‑check and system monitoring
functional.bounded_context: Legal Document Lifecycle Management (Deed Management, Archiving and Cryptographic Key Handling)
integrations: Relational Database via Spring Data JPA / Hibernate

debt_indicators: Very large number of Entity classes (360) suggests over‑modeling or lack of aggregation
Service layer contains DAO implementation classes (e.g., DeedEntryConnectionDaoImpl) indicating layered leakage
Controllers directly expose REST interfaces without a façade or use‑case layer
Potential God services – 42 service implementations, many likely handling multiple responsibilities
Missing explicit domain service interfaces; services are concrete classes only
High count of REST interface definitions (21) duplicated across controllers and services
recommendations: Introduce a use‑case / application service layer between Controllers and Domain Services to decouple REST handling from business logic
Group related entities into aggregate roots to reduce entity count and improve domain cohesion
Extract interfaces for all services and inject them; avoid concrete class references in Controllers
Refactor oversized services into smaller, single‑responsibility services (e.g., split ArchiveManagerServiceImpl, KeyManagerServiceImpl)
Apply proper repository abstraction: keep DAO implementations out of the Service layer and inject repositories via interfaces
Implement DTOs and mappers to separate persistence models (entities) from API models, reducing coupling between layers
Add package‑by‑feature modularization to enforce bounded context boundaries and prevent cross‑context leakage

container_analyses.overall_assessment: The backend provides comprehensive functionality for legal document lifecycle management but is hampered by architectural drift and technical debt. Moderate coupling and cohesion, together with a high volume of entities and monolithic services, limit scalability and maintainability. Addressing these issues with a clearer separation of concerns and domain‑driven design will substantially raise the quality grade.
top_recommendations: Add a dedicated use‑case/application service layer to isolate REST controllers from business logic.
Refactor large services into focused, single‑responsibility services and introduce interfaces for dependency injection.
Consolidate entities into aggregate roots and employ DTOs/mappers to decouple persistence models from API contracts.

executive_summary: uvz is a modular monolith system with 951 components across 2 active containers: frontend (404 components, Grade B), backend (494 components, Grade C). Overall architecture grade: C.
top_recommendations: Refactor overly large services into smaller, SRP‑compliant services.
Introduce façade/domain‑specific service layers to isolate UI components from low‑level adapters.
Consolidate and rationalize pipes and move complex transformations to services.

Review injection points to identify and eliminate unnecessary cross‑module dependencies
Add a dedicated use‑case/application service layer to isolate REST controllers from business logic.
